payload.text:
Make the follwowing changes :

remove and replace the Execute_Projects.exe to Download Resume only. 

i need the same button effect to the Download Resume. 




also, Remove the Github and Arrow link from both the Notable Projects.


also change the Notable Projects to accademic projects

**Evaluating Website Updates**

I've been evaluating changes for my portfolio website. Focusing on the hero section, I've decided to remove the "Execute\_Projects.exe" button but keep the resume download. I'm now looking to apply a particular visual style to the hero section, but it's proving tricky.


**Adapting Button Styles**

I'm now prioritizing button style consistency. I'm focusing on unifying the look of the "Download Resume" button with the "Execute_Projects.exe" button's visual style, removing the outline in favor of a solid accent color. I've also identified inconsistencies in the project section and plan to address them next.


**Implementing Arrow Link**

I'm now focusing on adding the arrow link to the "Notable Projects" section. I'm updating `components/Hero.tsx` to reflect the button change. The goal is to make the "Download Resume" button share the same visual style as the removed "Execute\_Projects.exe" button. Specifically, I'm working on applying the border and text accent, and ensuring a smooth transition effect on hover. The code now only includes the "Download Resume" button, with styling borrowed from the former button, and the desired content.


**Modifying Button & Titles**

I'm now implementing the "Download Resume" button, making sure it shares the same visual effect as the previously removed one. I've successfully applied the desired border, accent color for the text and a subtle download animation. My focus now is on updating `components/Projects.tsx`: I plan to rename the title from "Notable\_Projects" to "Academic\_Projects" and remove the header links from the project cards. I'm aiming for a cleaner and more streamlined presentation.


**Streamlining Project Display**

I'm now revising my project card display strategy. I've decided to remove the links from the project cards and reposition the folder icon within the card. This change eliminates unnecessary visual clutter. The "justify-between" utility will work as intended now that only the folder icon is present, maintaining a clean layout. I'll need to modify the component in `components/Hero.tsx` to accommodate this shift. I'm focusing on ensuring a consistent and refined visual presentation for all projects, and a cleaner presentation for the resume download button.


**Updating Project Titles**

I'm now focusing on `components/Projects.tsx`, specifically renaming "Notable_Projects" to "Academic_Projects" for clarity. I've also removed the header links from the project cards to reduce visual clutter, and updated the download button's visual design.


**Refining Button Animation**

I'm now revising the download button's hover effect. I've adjusted the animation so the arrow icon moves downwards on hover, aligning it with the intended design. The current code incorporates a refined style and the download attribute is now correctly included.  I'm now satisfied with the resume button's visual presentation.


**Optimizing Icon Consistency**

I'm now deciding on the best download icon. I'm choosing whether to use a consistent style, reusing the existing download button style with the standard icon, or implementing a new, more tailored design. I'm also reviewing `components/Projects.tsx` for any further required adjustments after the download icon revision.


**Revising Project Display**

I'm now simplifying the project card's header. I'm removing the inner `div` containing links to Github and external project sites from the project cards to reduce visual clutter and improve layout. I'll make sure the folder icon retains its current position, as well as updating the component name and header for the section.


**Updating Project Card Headers**

I'm now revising the project card header. I've removed the `div` containing the GitHub and external links, decluttering the visual layout and refining the design to focus on simplicity. I'm also ensuring the folder icon maintains its current position within the card for a cohesive look and feel. I've updated the section's name, as well.


**Finalizing Project Card Design**

I'm now completely removing the GitHub and external links from the project cards, keeping only the folder icon. This streamlines the display significantly. The section ID for "projects" remains unchanged so navigation is unaffected. I'm also changing the project section's display name from "Notable Projects" to "Academic Projects". The hero section is now simplified, with only the "Download Resume" button, styled to match the visual effect of the removed "Execute_Projects.exe" button.


**Finalizing Button Animations**

I've been revising the download button's animation, and I've decided to keep the hover effect subtle. I've ensured the arrow icon subtly moves downwards on hover, aligning it with the overall design. I'm now satisfied with the button's final visual presentation. The code is updated with this refined style and is ready for the website's deployment.


**Finalizing Project Card Design**

I'm now implementing the `components/Projects.tsx` changes. I'm focusing on completely removing the GitHub and external links, keeping only the folder icon within each project card for a cleaner look. The section ID for "projects" remains unchanged to preserve navigation functionality. I've also updated the project section's display name from "Notable Projects" to "Academic Projects." The hero section now features just the "Download Resume" button, styled to perfectly match the visual effect of the removed "Execute_Projects.exe" button.


**Updating Folder Icons**

I'm now focusing on the folder icon within the project cards. I've successfully adjusted the style of the folder icon, to match the rest of the project cards. I have updated the animation on the download button and tested it. I am satisfied with the change.
Here are the changes to update the Hero section buttons and the Projects section content and layout.

entries.description: Removed Execute Projects button, styled Download Resume button with the primary accent style, and removed unused ArrowRight import.
Renamed "Notable Projects" to "Academic Projects" and removed GitHub and External Link icons from project cards.

payload.text:
Update:

Replace Say hello to a forms and add Netlify form tags. 

the form should have the following:

The "Terminal" Interface (Classic Hacker)
Turn the contact form into a Linux-style terminal session.

The Look: Black background, bright green or neon blue monospaced font (e.g., 'Fira Code', 'Courier New').

The Input Fields:

Instead of boxes, use a blinking cursor prompt.

Label: Change "Name" to root@visitor:~$ Identity:

Label: Change "Email" to root@visitor:~$ Return_Address:

Label: Change "Message" to root@visitor:~$ Payload:

The Button:

Text: Instead of "Send," use ./execute_send.sh or [ INITIATE HANDSHAKE ].

Hover Effect: The text gltiches or inverts colors (black text on green block).
Micro-Interactions:

When the user clicks "Send," don't just clear the form. Show a Progress Bar that says: > Encrypting Payload... [||||||||||] 100% > Establishing Secure Connection... > Message Sent.

Input Validation: If they miss a field, instead of "Field required," display: ERROR: DATA CORRUPTION DETECTED. SECTOR EMPTY.

**Designing Contact Form**

I'm currently focused on the contact section of the portfolio. Replacing the existing "Say Hello" link with a Netlify-compatible form is the primary objective.  I've started sketching the form fields and am now determining the best approach for Netlify integration.


**Developing Terminal Form**

I've made considerable progress on the terminal-style contact form. The core form fields are styled to mimic a Linux terminal. I'm focusing on the button's appearance and micro-interactions for submission. A glitch effect on hover is planned, and the submission will show an encryption/connection progress bar with relevant messages.  Custom error messages are also now in the works for data validation.


**Crafting Form States**

I'm now fully immersed in developing the form's intricate states. I've defined states for inputs, status indicators (`idle`, `encrypting`, etc.), and handling validation errors. I've begun styling the input fields to seamlessly integrate with the terminal interface, removing borders and giving transparent backgrounds. I'm focusing on crafting the prompt text, that will reside left of each input, so that this contact form will really feel the part. The next step will be implementation of the submission logic, but I have already completed all the data validation.


**Refining Form Submission**

I'm currently finalizing the submission process. I'm leveraging Netlify's built-in form handling, with a hidden form tag in my `index.html`. In `Contact.tsx`, I'm employing the `encode` function for data encoding and fetching with the appropriate headers to submit the form data to Netlify. I will now integrate this with the submission animation I previously added to provide smooth feedback to the user on completion.


**Designing UI Elements**

I'm now focusing on designing the user interface elements for the contact form. I'm working on styling the terminal header, the input fields (including caret and text colors), and the progress bar. I'm also preparing to display error messages. I'm currently using a simple div for the progress bar with a width transition for animation. The error messages will appear below the input line or replace the prompt temporarily.


**Integrating Submission Feedback**

I'm now integrating the submission animation into the form. The "encrypting" state will trigger the progress bar, and upon reaching 100%, a "Establishing Secure Connection..." message appears, followed by the POST request. Upon successful submission, a "Message Sent" confirmation will be shown to the user. I've chosen the neon green from the theme, and implemented the necessary messages for the feedback area.


**Updating Encrypted Submission**

I'm now fully immersed in the animated submission flow. After the form is submitted, the `encrypting` state is immediately active, driving the progress bar animation. I've successfully connected the animation and the `setStatusLog`, to display the current status. The next state is `sending`, and then `success` or `error` depending on the request's result.


**Simulating Encryption Progress**

I'm currently focused on the `proceedToSend` function. I've successfully integrated a simulated encryption progress bar that increases in increments of 10% every 200 milliseconds. After the progress reaches 100%, the `proceedToSend` function is invoked to handle the secure connection attempt, along with relevant messages.


I'm currently creating the terminal form fields with labels that resemble a terminal prompt. I've decided to use a structure that includes a label and an input field in a flex container. I'm focusing on the appearance of the text in the input fields, and I plan to add the final form elements to the hidden form tag in the HTML.


**Designing Form Structure**

I'm currently structuring the form in `Contact.tsx`, keeping `name`, `email`, and `message` as the field names. The component will be rewritten for terminal form logic. I'm focusing on the visual details: a black/darker navy background, a monospace font, neon green prompts, white/slate user input, and an outline hover invert button. I've confirmed that the HTML form tag is sufficient for Netlify.


**Analyzing Prompt Requirements**

I've just recognized that the prompt contains several independent requests. The first part, concerning "Execute_Projects.exe" and the download resume button, appears to apply to `Hero.tsx`, but there's a disconnect; the given `Hero.tsx` doesn't have an "Execute Projects" button. I'm now clarifying the original user's intent to resolve this discrepancy. I'll need to confirm if I should be removing or replacing anything in that component.


**Confirming Prompt Intent**

I'm now clarifying the original user's intent to resolve this discrepancy. The `Hero.tsx` *did* include "Execute Projects" functionality and was modified according to my previous summary, but I'm unsure if the user wants me to revert these changes. I'll need confirmation on whether to restore the original "Execute Projects" button.


**Analyzing Recent Changes**

I've just reviewed the discrepancies between the provided instructions and the current state of the code. The `Hero.tsx` now correctly includes the "Download Resume" button and no longer has "Execute Projects." Also, the "Notable Projects" section in `Projects.tsx` has been successfully updated to "Academic Projects," and the GitHub and external links have been removed, with only the folder icon remaining. Now I'm shifting focus to implementing the new terminal-style contact form with Netlify form integration, as previously described.


**Revising Netlify Integration**

I'm currently revising the Netlify form integration. I've re-reviewed the Netlify documentation and made some adjustments to the `Contact.tsx` component. I'm focusing on the `encode` function and the necessary headers to submit the form data correctly. I'm also preparing to add error handling and success messages to provide feedback to the user upon submission.

**Enhancing Terminal Display**

I'm now fully immersed in developing the terminal interface's visual aspects. The styling for the wrapper, header, and form fields is nearly complete, and I'm adding micro-animations for an interactive experience. The button interaction is in progress, and the progress bar's behavior is under testing. The logging logic is mostly completed, ensuring proper feedback to the user, with custom messages for each event.


**Updating Encryption Progress**

I'm now integrating mock encryption animations into the terminal interface. I've designed a sequence of messages displayed as a progress bar simulates encryption. After the bar is complete, it transitions to a secure connection attempt. This is now fully integrated into the code, and messages are displayed in the form's feedback area. The next step is data submission after the secure connection has been established.


**Adjusting Social Integration**

I'm now integrating social icons into the contact section, preserving their placement beneath the terminal form. The original text intro will also remain, ensuring the overall aesthetic and flow. I'm prioritizing seamless visual integration of these elements.


**Refining Icon Imports**

I'm presently optimizing the icon imports in `Contact.tsx`. I've confirmed the `getSocialIcon` function's logic and the necessary icons: `AlertTriangle`, `CheckCircle`, and `Terminal`, while removing `Twitter` from the initial import list as it's not present in the `SOCIALS` constant. I'll maintain the existing logic for managing social icons in the component.


**Streamlining Icon Usage**

I'm presently optimizing the icon usage in `Contact.tsx`. I've ensured a cohesive set of social icons, accurately reflecting the `SOCIALS` constant in `constants.ts`, and removing unused imports. The focus is on clean and maintainable imports for seamless social integration within the component.


**Enhancing Error Handling**

I'm presently optimizing the input change and submission error handling. The input change now resets the form status to "idle" and clears any error message. I've streamlined data validation checks before submission and incorporated more specific error messages for missing fields. I'm focusing on providing more informative feedback in the terminal log area.


**Optimizing Form Submission**

I'm now fully focused on the submission process within the component. The code now iterates through a series of steps simulating encryption and connection establishment messages, updating the terminal log with progress. After a successful connection the `fetch` is executed to submit the data, then updates the status of the form to either `success` or `error` depending on the result, and logs relevant messages to the terminal.


**Improving Error Messages**

I'm now refining the error messages displayed in the terminal. I'm focusing on providing clear and concise feedback to the user when input validation fails, or when a submission error occurs. I'm working to make the terminal's feedback more informative and helpful.


**Implementing Terminal Feedback**

I'm now implementing the final feedback mechanisms for the terminal-style contact form. The terminal should provide clear confirmation upon submission. I've successfully incorporated error messages for invalid input.  Next, I'll be implementing `success` and `error` states for the form, which will display dynamic messages during successful and unsuccessful requests.


**Improving Input Handling**

I'm now refining the input handling for the terminal contact form. Specifically, I'm integrating the provided HTML input tags, like the `data-netlify="true"` attribute.  I am making sure that the input fields have the correct `name` attribute and the `value` is connected with the state, through the `onChange` event, to the `formData` object. I am also working on connecting the `onSubmit` to the appropriate `handleSubmit` function. Finally, the conditional rendering of the `disabled` property, depending on the status, has also been added.


**Optimizing Input Handling**

I'm presently optimizing the input handling for the terminal contact form. I've integrated HTML input tags with attributes and handling `onChange` events. Conditional rendering of the `disabled` property, depending on the status, has also been added. The `onSubmit` attribute connects to the `handleSubmit` function.


**Enhancing Terminal Feedback**

I'm working on the `Terminal.tsx` component, focusing on the `TerminalLog` to enhance its feedback. I'm dynamically updating the `logs` state. I've designed logic to display a clear success message after a successful submission, enhancing user confirmation. I'm refining the handling of potential submission errors and displaying specific error messages for user clarity.


**Improving Terminal Display**

I'm now focusing on the `Terminal.tsx` component, specifically refining the `TerminalLog` for enhanced feedback. I'm dynamically managing the `logs` state. I've successfully added logic to display a clear success message after a successful submission, providing users with explicit confirmation. I'm now refining the handling of potential submission errors, to provide more specific messages.


**Integrating Submission Button**

I've completed integrating the animated submission button into the form, along with its conditional rendering and disabled state management. The button design aligns with the terminal theme, with micro-interactions on hover and while submitting. The `disabled` state is now correctly linked to the form status, ensuring appropriate user feedback, and is fully styled as well.


**Updating Resume Button**

I'm now implementing the resume button. The styling and effects from the "Execute Projects" button are being replicated on the "Download Resume" button, as specified. This component is now complete and will provide the final visual details.


**Revising Netlify Integration**

I'm revisiting the Netlify form handling to refine the data encoding and headers in `Contact.tsx`. I'll ensure the form data is encoded correctly using `encodeURIComponent` to comply with Netlify's requirements. I'm focusing on crafting a comprehensive set of error and success messages for clearer user feedback and seamless integration into the terminal UI.


**Integrating Remaining Icons**

I'm now integrating the final icons into the contact form's design. The `ChevronRight`, `Check`, `AlertTriangle`, `Terminal`, and `Globe` icons are now successfully incorporated into the `Contact.tsx` component. The final XML construction is in progress.
Here are the updates to the `Contact.tsx` component to implement the Linux-terminal style form with the requested features.

entries.description: Replaced "Say Hello" button with a fully functional, terminal-themed Netlify contact form. Features include CLI-style prompts, typing simulation, progress animation, and error handling with custom system messages.

payload.text:
one more thing to add.

i need a form validation on email address entered on root@visitor:~$ Return_Address:

it should be a valid email id

**Validating Email Addresses**

I'm currently working on adding email validation to the "Return_Address" field in `Contact.tsx`. Building upon existing validation, which already checks for empty fields and provides a custom error message, I'm now integrating an email format check to ensure data integrity.


**Integrating Email Validation**

I've located `components/Contact.tsx` and am updating the `handleSubmit` function. My focus now is on integrating a regex check for the email format before allowing form submission. If the email fails validation, I'll set the status to 'error' and update the `errorMessage` appropriately to signal the issue. I'm aiming for a concise and informative error message for the user.


**Adding Error Messages**

I've implemented the email validation logic within the `handleSubmit` function in `components/Contact.tsx`. I decided on the error message "ERROR: RETURN ADDRESS MALFORMED." to maintain consistency with the other error messages. Now, when the user provides an invalid email, the form will display this specific error. I'm satisfied that this is complete and the form's logic appears robust and well-integrated.


**Refining Validation Logic**

I'm now focusing on refining the email validation process within `Contact.tsx`. I'm going to expand the existing email validation to provide more specific error feedback. I am building a set of unit tests to ensure that the form validation is robust, and covers a wide range of input errors. The goal is a more user-friendly experience, with helpful error messages guiding the user to correct their input. I'm aiming for detailed validation.


**Updating Email Validation**

I've integrated a robust email validation function to refine user input checks in the "Return_Address" field. This function uses a comprehensive regex pattern to validate the email format thoroughly. When the format is deemed invalid, I generate a specific error message signaling this issue, which helps the user understand the exact problem. I have successfully integrated this logic into the form, aiming for an improved user experience.


**Optimizing Form Submission**

I am now focusing on refining the form submission process after successful email validation. The current task involves integrating a more graceful 'success' state, providing informative logs, and handling potential network errors more robustly. I'm carefully reviewing the error-handling logic and will be integrating comprehensive logging to aid debugging. I intend to enhance the feedback mechanism to improve the user experience post-submission.


**Finalizing the Contact Form**

I've finished the form, focusing on graceful integration and robust submission. I've incorporated clear feedback for successful submissions and enhanced the overall error-handling. The "Contact" section is now complete, providing a secure and user-friendly experience.  I'm pleased with the form's functionality and its role in connecting users.


**Reviewing Contact Module**

I'm now revisiting the "contact_module.sh" script to refine its role within the overall system.  I will be integrating it in such a way that it gracefully interacts with the rest of the app, ensuring consistency in the user experience.  I'm focusing on ensuring it is correctly integrated.

**Enhancing Input Fields**

I'm now integrating new inputs for the contact form. I'm focusing on "Identity" and "Email," creating consistent field labels, and adjusting their attributes. I aim to improve form aesthetics while implementing user input safeguards. I'm adding `autoComplete="off"` to the "Identity" field for privacy. I'm also preparing for the email integration.


**Refining Form Fields**

I'm now revising the 'Email' field within the contact form. I'm focusing on ensuring it handles user input gracefully, particularly regarding the display of placeholders and handling changes. I am also planning to improve the overall design of the field to better match the style of the rest of the form. The goal is to create a seamless and user-friendly experience for data entry.


**Updating Display Logic**

I'm now focusing on the display logic for the form's status, specifically the error messages and success notifications. I'm reviewing the implementation and considering improvements to the visual cues and their responsiveness. I'm working to provide a more intuitive and informative user experience with updated feedback. I plan to enhance error-message emphasis.


**Adjusting Form States**

I'm presently refactoring the form submission button and integrating a more streamlined state management system. I'm focusing on clarifying the interactions with success, error, and idle states to improve user flow. I intend to enhance the visual feedback and make it clear to the user when the button is actively submitting, processing an error, or ready for a new session. I am optimizing the button's behavior.


**Improving Button Interaction**

I'm now overhauling the form submission button. I want to enhance user feedback during various states like `submitting`, `error`, or `success`. I'm integrating smoother transitions and clearer visual cues to improve user understanding. I'm focusing on the `./execute_send.sh` button. I'm aiming for enhanced form feedback.


**Updating Social Links**

I'm now focusing on integrating the social media links into the contact form. I'm aiming for a visually appealing and functional display, ensuring smooth transitions and clear icons. I'm carefully reviewing the existing code for the social media section and plan to incorporate it seamlessly into the new contact module. I am focusing on smooth integration.

Here is the update to the contact form to include email validation.

# Updated files:

-   Added `validateEmail` function using regex.
-   Updated `handleSubmit` to check the email format before processing the form.
